revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23986660476|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:هوندن (شنتو)]][[ca:Honden]][[cs:Honden]][[en:Honden]][[es:Honden]][[eu:Honden]][[fi:Honden]][[fr:Honden]][[id:Honden]][[it:Honden]][[ja:本殿]][[nl:Honden (shinto)]][[oc:Honden]][[pl:Honden]][[pt:Honden]][[ru:Хондэн]][[simple:Honden]][[th:ฮนเด็ง]][[tr:Honden]]
{{Short description|Main hall of a Shinto shrine}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:IzumoTaisha Honden (detail).jpg|thumb|250 px|[[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]]'s {{lang|ja-latn|honden}}, closed to the public]] In {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} architecture, the {{nihongo|'''''honden'''''| 本殿|main hall}}, also called {{nihongo|''shinden''|神殿}}, or sometimes {{nihongo|''shōden''|昇殿}} as in [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]]'s case, is the most sacred building at a Shinto shrine, intended purely for the use of the enshrined ''[[kami]]'', usually symbolized by a mirror or sometimes by a statue.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref><ref>''Shinden'', [http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/540747/shinden "Shinden"] ''Encyclopædia Britannica''</ref> The building is normally in the rear of the shrine and closed to the general public.<ref name="mori"/> In front of it usually stands the ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or [[Oratory (worship)|oratory]]. The ''haiden'' is often connected to the ''honden'' by a ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or hall of offerings.<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/heiden.htm Heiden], ''Encyclopedia of Shinto'', accessed don November 17, 2009</ref>
Physically, the ''honden'' is the heart of the shrine complex, connected to the rest of the shrine but usually raised above it, and protected from public access by a fence called ''{{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|qid=Q3078312|lt=Tamagaki|1=Sacred fences}}''. It usually is relatively small and with a gabled roof. Its doors are usually kept closed, except at [[Japanese festivals|religious festivals]]. [[Kannushi|Shinto priest]]s themselves enter only to perform rituals.<ref name="jaanus"/> The rite of opening those doors is itself an important part of the shrine's life.<ref name="mori">Mori Mizue</ref> Inside the ''honden'' is kept the {{nihongo|''[[Shintai|go-shintai]]''|御神体}}, literally, "the sacred body of the kami". The ''go-shintai'' is actually not divine, but just a temporary repository of the enshrined ''kami''.<ref>Smyers (1999:44)</ref>
Important as it is, the ''honden'' may sometimes be completely absent, as for example when the shrine stands on a sacred mountain to which it is dedicated, or when there are nearby ''[[himorogi]]'' (enclosure) or other ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}'' (substitute object) that serve as a more direct bond to a ''kami''.<ref name="mori"/> {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, for example, contains no sacred images or objects because it is believed to serve the mountain on which it stands ({{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}).<ref>See [http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/eng.html Ōmiwa Shrine site]</ref><ref name="Tamura20">Tamura, page 21</ref> For the same reason, it has a {{nihongo|''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]''|拝殿|worship hall}}, but no ''honden''. In this sense, it is a model of what the first Shinto shrines were like.<ref name="Tamura20"/>
Another important shrine without a ''honden'' is [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Taisha]], head of the Suwa shrine network.
The ''honden's'' structure determines the shrine's architectural style. Many exist, but three (''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'') are of particular importance because they are the only ones believed to predate the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism in Japan}}, and have therefore a special architectural and historical significance. They are exemplified respectively by the ''honden'' at [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]]. German architect {{ill|Bruno Taut|en|Bruno Taut|qid=Q76729|lt=Bruno Taut|1=Bruno Taut}} compared the importance of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]]'s ''honden'' to that of Greece's {{ill|Parthenon|en|Parthenon|qid=Q10288|lt=Parthenon|1=Parthenon}}. For details, see the article {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}}.
== See also == * {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} ''(Hondō)'' of a temple for the similar concept in {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture * {{ill|Holy of Holies|en|Holy of Holies|qid=Q3620148|lt=Holy of Holies|1=Holy of Holies}} in Judeo-Christian traditions
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
==References== * {{cite book|last=Tamura|first=Yoshiro|title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History|publisher=Kosei Publishing Company|location=Tokyo|year=2000|edition=First|chapter=The Birth of the Japanese nation in|isbn=4-333-01684-3|page=232 pages}} * {{cite web |url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ |title=Honden |publisher=JAANUS |access-date=2008-12-19 }} * {{cite encyclopedia |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=250 |title=Honden |last=Mori |first=Mizue |date=2005-06-02 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2008-12-19 }} * {{cite book | last = Smyers, Karen Ann | author-link = Karen Ann Smyers | title = The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1999 | location = Honolulu | isbn = 0-8248-2102-5 | oclc = 231775156 }}
{{Commons Category|Honden}}
